The basic topology of the inverter is shown in figure 1.16 which is feeding a three phase asynchronous motor. The inverter converts the DC to AC through power electronic. The converter consists of the three legs with IGBT transistors, and free-wheeling diodes. The inverter is supplied by a voltage source U0 is the DC voltage [3]. Six switches are controlled by Pulse width modulation (PWM).it is a very common control method used for the voltage source inverter to obtain variable voltage and frequency supply by adjusting the pulse width. The circuit is operated by switching Sa, Sb, Sc. The inverter uses two pairs of complementary controlled switches in each inverter phase or leg, as shown in figure 1.16. Considering that the two switches in each inverter phase or leg operate in a balancing pair in order to avoid short circuiting the DC source [10][11] [meng2017].

The basic topology of the inverter shown in Figure 1.16 feeds a three-phase asynchronous motor by converting DC to AC power using power electronics. The converter has three legs with IGBT transistors and free-wheeling diodes. The inverter receives DC voltage supply, U0, and is controlled using pulse width modulation (PWM) which is a common method to obtain variable voltage and frequency supply by adjusting pulse width. The circuit operates by switching Sa, Sb, and Sc. The inverter consists of two pairs of complementary controlled switches in each inverter phase or leg to avoid short circuiting the DC source.

PI control has been widely used as a cascaded form of control in variable-speed motor drives. The PI controllers applied to speed of the DTC drive and to the flux, torque and speed of the DTC-SVM drive. It was assumed that the linear models represent a good approximation of the real system. Hence, metaheuristic techniques were applied for choosing the controller gains offline, based on optimization problems. For the speed loop, the Symmetrical Optimum Criterion was considered for tuning the gains. Such approach was validated considering simulations with the complete nonlinear model and practical experiments with the real system. In the speed control mode, a PID controller is used, the input of which is the error between the reference speed and the actual speed of the motor. The anti-windup is in function when saturation occurred to correct the integral action of the regulator. [achalhi2018] [15][16][jnayah2020][10.1016@j.asoc.2017.09.007].

PI control is widely used as a cascaded form of control in variable-speed motor drives. This approach involves applying PI controllers to the speed of the DTC drive and to the flux, torque, and speed of the DTC-SVM drive. Linear models were used as approximations of the real system, and metaheuristic techniques were applied offline to optimize the controller gains. For the speed loop, the Symmetrical Optimum Criterion was used to tune the gains. This approach was validated through simulations with the complete nonlinear model and practical experiments with the real system. In the speed control mode, a PID controller is used, with the input being the error between the reference speed and the actual speed of the motor. Anti-windup is utilized in the event of saturation to correctly adjust the integral action of the regulator. These findings are supported by works cited from Achalhi, Jnayah, and others. [achalhi2018] [15][16][jnayah2020][10.1016@j.asoc.2017.09.007].

The SVM method is implemented to generate pulses for the DC-AC inverter switches. This approach has several advantages, including a reduced number of switch commutations, lower harmonic content in the output voltage, a greater modulation index leading to better DC bus voltage, and easy implementation in microprocessors. The SVM algorithm used in this paper is based on an approach initially proposed by Kim and Sul in 1996. This approach has been further developed by Kanchan, Baiju, Mohapatra, Ouseph, and Gopakumar in 2005 and by Krishna, Amarnath, and Kamakshiah in 2012, particularly in terms of hardware implementation. [10.1016@j.conengprac.2019.05.003]

The SVM-DTC operates at constant switching frequency. The DTC-SVM scheme presented in Fig. 1 has speed, torque and flux loops operating with PI controllers. [10.1016@j.conengprac.2019.05.003] [10.1016@j.asoc.2017.09.007]. the motor speed (ω_r) is sensed using a speed sensor, which converts the mechanical speed into an equivalent voltage value compared with the reference speed(ω_r^* ) . The speed error is processed through a PI controller, generating the reference torque (Te*). The reference torque is compared with the estimated torque (Te), generating the torque error (ΔTe), which is handled through a torque PI controller (Vq*). The estimated flux (Φs), is compared with the reference flux (Φs*), and the error (ΔΦs) is processed through a flux PI controller, generating (Vd*).Ones (Vd*,Vq*) are obtained, the transformation of variables from the synchronous to stationary frame (α-β) using (θ_s) estimator, then injected into the SVM modulation block which generates the inverter control orders. Equations  (1) and (2) describe the calculation to obtain Currents (Ia,Ib,Ic) and voltage (Va,Vb,Vc) generated form inverter transformed into the stationary frame (d-q) to generate (Vsd,Vsq,Isd,Isq) that used to estimate torque, flux and sector (Te, Φs, θ_s) [1] [3,33,37-38]. [sudheer2017]

The SVM-DTC operates at a constant switching frequency, while the DTC-SVM scheme illustrated in Figure 1 has speed, torque, and flux loops operating with PI controllers. Motor speed (ω_r) is sensed using a speed sensor that converts the mechanical speed into a corresponding voltage value compared with the reference speed (ω_r^*). The speed error is processed through a PI controller to generate the reference torque (Te*). The reference torque is compared with the estimated torque (Te) to generate the torque error (ΔTe), which is processed through a torque PI controller (Vq*). The estimated flux (Φs) is compared with the reference flux (Φs*), and the error (ΔΦs) is processed through a flux PI controller to generate (Vd*). Once (Vd*,Vq*) are obtained, a transformation of variables from the synchronous to stationary frame (α-β) using a (θ_s) estimator is carried out before injection into the SVM modulation block to generate the inverter control orders. Equations (1) and (2) describe the calculation to obtain the currents (Ia,Ib,Ic) and voltage (Va,Vb,Vc) generated from the inverter, which are then transformed into the stationary frame (d-q) to estimate torque, flux, and sector (Te, Φs, θ_s), as supported by works cited from Sudheer and others. [10.1016@j.conengprac.2019.05.003] [10.1016@j.asoc.2017.09.007] [1] [3,33,37-38].

As shown in Figure 1, there are two control loops corresponding to the stator flux and torque magnitudes. The reference values (Te*,Φs*) for the torque and the flux compared with the actual values (Φs, Te) to induce errors (∆Φs,∆Te). These errors feed into two hysteresis blocks, respectively. The outputs from the two hysteresis blocks and the stator flux position (S) are used as inputs to the switching table. The inverter is switched on using these errors and the position of the stator flux via six-region control. This results in the inverter operating in six-step operation with six active vectors and two zero vectors, as shown in Figure 3. The inverter output voltage vector is adjusted to minimize the flux and torque errors and define the direction of the flux rotation. [8599] [25] [29-31].

Figure 1 illustrates two control loops corresponding to the stator flux and torque magnitudes. The reference values for torque and flux (Te*, Φs*) are compared with actual values (Φs, Te), inducing errors (∆Φs, ∆Te) that feed into two hysteresis blocks. The outputs from these hysteresis blocks, as well as the stator flux position (S), serve as inputs to the switching table. Using these errors and the position of the stator flux, the inverter is switched on via six-region control. As a result, the inverter operates in six-step operation with six active vectors and two zero vectors, as shown in Figure 3. The inverter output voltage vector is adjusted to minimize flux and torque errors and define the direction of the flux rotation, as demonstrated by previous works cited from sources numbered 8599, 25, 29-31.

Direct Torque Control (DTC) and Space Vector Modulated-Direct Torque Control (SVM-DTC) [2,3]  are the latest research technologies for the VSI fed cage IMs [978-3-319-44944-9 33.pdf ] widely used in permanent magnet synchronous motor control with its simple control mode, fast torque response, and strong perturbation of internal parameters and external disturbance [1]. It has been examined during the last decade in the area of AC drives. This control strategy was confirmed by Takahashi in 1986 [2]. Many papers [3-5] have discussed the modelling of inverters and induction motors.

Direct Torque Control (DTC) and Space Vector Modulated-Direct Torque Control (SVM-DTC) are the latest technologies for Variable Speed Inverter (VSI) fed cage induction motors, as discussed in research papers such as [978-3-319-44944-9 33.pdf]. These control strategies have been widely used in the control of permanent magnet synchronous motors due to their simple control mode, fast torque response, and strong performance against perturbations of internal parameters and external disturbances. This control strategy was first proposed by Takahashi in 1986 and has been extensively studied in the field of AC drives in the last decade. Several papers, such as those cited in [3-5], have discussed the modelling of inverters and induction motors to support these control strategies.

Many artificial intelligence (AI) techniques have been developed and used to control induction motors. Fuzzy logic control (FLC) [5-9], adaptive neuro-fuzzy interference system (ANFIS) [10-12], and artificial neural networks (ANN) [13-16] are examples of such systems.

There has been considerable development and application of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques for induction motor control. Fuzzy logic control (FLC) [5-9], adaptive neuro-fuzzy interference system (ANFIS) [10-12], and artificial neural networks (ANN) [13-16] are some examples of these systems. These techniques have been developed to address issues such as robustness, noise rejection, and non-linearities that exist in induction motor control systems. They have been demonstrated to provide improved performance compared to traditional control methods in some cases. Many research studies have been conducted to assess their effectiveness in different induction motor control scenarios.

Many faults occur in different elements in the AC drive, such as stator winding faults [19–20], cut of an inverter arm fault [21], switching device, DC bus faults, faults in the machine (stator fault, rotor), faults in the drive chain (mating defect, sensors), power supply faults (imbalance of power supply, fault in the power electronics converter). [22], as well as a fault tolerance control [21].Given this, switching devices in the study are likely to be damaged by faults due to their frailty [18,23,24].Various fault diagnosis schemes used in electrical machine drives have been developed and published [25-28].

Many different faults can occur in different elements of an AC drive, including stator winding faults [19-20], inverter arm cut-off faults [21], switching device failures, DC bus faults, faults in the machine (e.g., stator and rotor faults), faults in the drive chain (e.g., defects in mating or sensors), power supply faults (e.g., power supply imbalance, faults in the power electronics converter), and fault tolerance control [21]. It is important to note that switching devices in the AC drive are particularly vulnerable to faults due to their fragility, as has been discussed in previous research studies [18, 23-24]. Various fault diagnosis schemes have been developed and published for electrical machine drives [25-28]. These schemes aim to detect and identify faults promptly to enable timely repairs and prevent further damage to the system.

in this chapter we implement tow kind of feature extractions methods (sum algabrique and Fast Fourier Transform) to study the possibility of fault finding and diagnosis of short and open circuit faults inverter by using neural networks and Fuzzy logic control. A defect classification based on artificial neural networks and Fuzzy logic control will be set to allow detection and localization of faults in the PWM inverter.	[ER_instructions-OTH-OTH (1)]
The chapter outlines the Direct Torque Control (DTC) principle of the Induction Motor (IM) in the second section. The third section is dedicated to introducing the DTC-SVM. Section four describes the concept of feature extraction for diagnosing short and open circuit faults in inverters (as well as for identifying their types and locations). The fifth section involves the application of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) to diagnose issues in both DTC and DTC-SVM-IM settings. In the sixth section, the simulation results of the studied control topology are presented. Finally, conclusion.

This chapter presents two feature extraction methods (sum algebraic and Fast Fourier Transform) to investigate the possibility of fault finding and diagnosis for short and open circuit faults in the inverter using neural networks and Fuzzy logic control. To enable the detection and localization of these faults in the PWM inverter, a defect classification system based on artificial neural networks and Fuzzy logic control is proposed. The second section of the chapter outlines the principle of Direct Torque Control (DTC) for Induction Motors (IMs). The third section introduces the DTC-SVM, while the fourth section describes the concept of feature extraction for diagnosing short and open circuit faults in inverters and identifying their types and locations. Section five applies Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) to diagnose issues in both DTC and DTC-SVM-IM settings. In the sixth section, simulation results for the studied control topology are presented. Finally, the chapter concludes.

in this chapter we implement tow kind of feature extractions methods (sum algabrique and Fast Fourier Transform) to study the possibility of fault finding and diagnosis of short and open circuit faults inverter by using neural networks and Fuzzy logic control. A defect classification based on artificial neural networks and Fuzzy logic control will be set to allow detection and localization of faults in the PWM inverter.	[ER_instructions-OTH-OTH (1)]

This chapter explores the possibility of fault finding and diagnosis for short and open circuit faults in the inverter by implementing two feature extraction methods: sum algebraic and Fast Fourier Transform. To achieve this goal, the chapter utilizes neural networks and Fuzzy logic control. A defect classification system based on artificial neural networks and Fuzzy logic control will be established to allow detection and localization of faults in the PWM inverter.

The chapter outlines the Direct Torque Control (DTC) principle of the Induction Motor (IM) in the second section. The third section is dedicated to introducing the DTC-SVM. Section four describes the concept of feature extraction for diagnosing short and open circuit faults in inverters (as well as for identifying their types and locations). The fifth section involves the application of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) to diagnose issues in both DTC and DTC-SVM-IM settings. In the sixth section, the simulation results of the studied control topology are presented. Finally, conclusion.

The second section of the chapter outlines the Direct Torque Control (DTC) principle for the Induction Motor (IM). The third section introduces DTC-SVM. Section four describes the feature extraction concept for diagnosing short and open circuit faults in inverters, including methods for identifying the types and locations of these faults. In section five, the chapter applies Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) to diagnose issues in both DTC and DTC-SVM-IM settings. In the sixth section, simulation results for the studied control topology are presented. Finally, the chapter concludes.
